questions.question: Explain the purpose and prototype of mkdir() system call.
keyPoints: mkdir() creates a new directory
Prototype: int mkdir(const char *pathname, mode_t mode)
Returns 0 on success, -1 on error
Requires <sys/stat.h> header

questions.question: What are the conditions for successfully removing a directory using rmdir()?
keyPoints: Directory must exist
Directory must be empty
Directory must not be current working directory
Returns 0 on success, -1 on failure

questions.question: Explain the difference between mkdir() and mkdir -p command in shell.
keyPoints: mkdir() creates single-level directory
mkdir -p creates parent directories as needed
mkdir() fails if parent doesn't exist
mkdir -p succeeds even if parent directories don't exist

questions.question: What is umask and how does it affect directory permissions?
keyPoints: umask is a user file creation mask
It masks off certain permission bits
Directories created with mode & ~umask & 0777
Default umask is usually 022, resulting in 755 permissions

questions.question: Explain the special directory entries '.' and '..'.
keyPoints: . refers to the current directory
.. refers to the parent directory
Automatically created in every new directory
Used for path navigation and relative path resolution

questions.question: List at least four error conditions that can cause mkdir() to fail.
keyPoints: EEXIST: Directory already exists
ENOENT: Parent directory doesn't exist
ENOTDIR: Component of path is not a directory
EACCES: Permission denied
EPERM: Operation not permitted
ENOSPC: No space left on device

questions.question: Write a C program to create a directory named 'mydir' with permissions 0755.
keyPoints: Include required headers
Use mkdir() with pathname and mode
Check return value for error handling
Print success or error message

questions.question: Write a C program that creates a directory hierarchy /home/user/documents/projects using mkdir(). Explain why standard mkdir() would fail and how to handle this.
keyPoints: Standard mkdir() creates only one level
Would fail because /home/user/documents doesn't exist
Solution 1: Create each level sequentially
Solution 2: Use system() with mkdir -p command
Include proper error handling for each call
Show complete working code

questions.question: Explain the concept of directory permissions in UNIX/Linux. How does mkdir() handle the mode parameter? What is the role of umask?
keyPoints: Directories have owner, group, other permissions
mode parameter specifies permissions (e.g., 0755)
umask masks off certain bits during creation
Default umask 022 results in 755 permissions
Permissions can be changed with chmod() later
Process must have write permission on parent directory

questions.question: Write a C program to safely remove an empty directory with proper error handling. Include checks for existence, emptiness, and whether it is the current directory.
keyPoints: Check if directory exists using stat() or access()
Attempt rmdir() and check return value
Handle specific errors: ENOENT, ENOTEMPTY, EINVAL/EBUSY
Use proper errno checking with perror() or strerror()
Explain each error condition to user

questions.question: Compare and contrast mkdir() and rmdir() system calls. Include their prototypes, return values, parameters, error conditions, and header files required.
keyPoints: mkdir() creates directories, rmdir() removes them
Both return int (0 success, -1 failure)
mkdir() takes pathname and mode; rmdir() takes only pathname
mkdir() requires <sys/stat.h>; rmdir() requires <unistd.h>
Common errors: ENOENT (path doesn't exist), EACCES (permission denied)
rmdir() specific: ENOTEMPTY; mkdir() specific: EEXIST
